title: Quiz 1
subtitle: B1 Grammar Mix
source: Converted from Claude Daniel 4.27 Eng B1 50.html

questions.question: I ___ never been to Paris before. I'd love to visit one day!
questions.explanation: Present perfect uses 'have/has + past participle'. With I/you/we/they, we use 'have'.

questions.question: My sister ___ to Spain on holiday last summer.
questions.explanation: With a finished time like 'last summer', we use the past simple ('went'), not the present perfect.

questions.question: While I ___ a book, the phone suddenly rang.
questions.explanation: Past continuous (was/were + -ing) describes a longer action in progress when something else happened.

questions.question: When I got home, my little brother ___ his homework.
questions.explanation: He was in the middle of doing homework when I arrived — past continuous fits the longer background action.

questions.question: We ___ in this house for ten years now.
questions.explanation: Present perfect with 'for' shows something that started in the past and continues to now.

questions.question: I have known my best friend ___ 2018.
questions.explanation: 'Since' is used with a starting point (a date, year, time). 'For' is used with a length of time.

questions.question: When I was little, I ___ play with toy dinosaurs every day.
questions.explanation: 'Used to + verb' describes past habits or situations that are no longer true.

questions.question: She ___ a beautiful song at the school concert yesterday.
questions.explanation: Irregular verb: sing → sang (past simple) → sung (past participle).

questions.question: Look at those dark clouds! It ___ rain in a minute.
questions.explanation: For predictions based on something we can see now, we use 'be going to'.

questions.question: That's the phone ringing. Don't worry, I ___ answer it.
questions.explanation: For a decision made at the moment of speaking, we use 'will'.

questions.question: You look really tired. You ___ get some rest.
questions.explanation: 'Should' gives advice — it suggests what is a good idea.

questions.question: This is a library, so everyone ___ stay quiet.
questions.explanation: 'Must' shows strong obligation — something it is necessary to do.

questions.question: I'm not sure about my plans. I ___ go to the beach tomorrow.
questions.explanation: 'Might' shows that something is possible but not certain.

questions.question: Tomorrow is Saturday, so I ___ go to school. I can sleep in!
questions.explanation: 'Don't have to' = it isn't necessary. 'Mustn't' = it's forbidden. There's a big difference!

questions.question: When my mum was six years old, she ___ already swim very well.
questions.explanation: 'Could' is the past form of 'can' for ability.

questions.question: If it ___ tomorrow, we'll stay at home and watch a movie.
questions.explanation: First conditional: 'If + present simple, … will + verb'. Don't use 'will' in the if-part!

questions.question: Water ___ at 100 degrees Celsius — that's a fact.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional uses present simple in BOTH parts. We use it for facts that are always true.

questions.question: If I ___ a million dollars, I would buy a big house with a garden.
questions.explanation: Second conditional (imaginary situation): 'If + past simple, … would + verb'.

questions.question: If I ___ you, I would tell the truth.
questions.explanation: In second conditional, we say 'if I were' (not 'was'), even for 'I'. It's a special form for imaginary situations.

questions.question: English ___ in many countries around the world.
questions.explanation: Present passive: 'is/are + past participle'. Use it when the action matters more than who does it.

questions.question: The Mona Lisa ___ by Leonardo da Vinci a long time ago.
questions.explanation: Past passive: 'was/were + past participle'. The Mona Lisa is the subject; Leonardo did the action.

questions.question: Pizza ___ in Italy a long time ago.
questions.explanation: We don't say WHO invented it — we just say it WAS invented. That's why we use the passive.

questions.question: He said: 'I am tired.' → He said he ___ tired.
questions.explanation: In reported speech, present simple usually changes to past simple: am/is → was, are → were.

questions.question: Tom said: 'I have lost my keys.' → Tom said he ___ his keys.
questions.explanation: Present perfect ('have lost') changes to past perfect ('had lost') in reported speech.

questions.question: She asked me ___ I had a pet at home.
questions.explanation: For yes/no questions in reported speech, we use 'if' (or 'whether').

questions.question: I have a friend ___ lives in Australia.
questions.explanation: We use 'who' for people, 'which' for things, 'where' for places.

questions.question: This is the camera ___ I bought yesterday at the shop.
questions.explanation: 'Which' refers to things. (You can also use 'that' here — both are fine.)

questions.question: He's the boy ___ father is a famous singer.
questions.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession — 'his father' becomes 'whose father'.

questions.question: My older sister is really good at ___ cakes.
questions.explanation: After a preposition (like 'at', 'of', 'for'), we always use the -ing form.

questions.question: For my birthday, I'd like ___ a new bicycle.
questions.explanation: After 'would like', 'want', 'need', 'decide', we use 'to + verb' (the infinitive).

questions.question: I really enjoy ___ films at the cinema with my friends.
questions.explanation: After 'enjoy', 'like', 'love', 'hate', 'finish', 'mind', we use the -ing form.

questions.question: ___ he was very tired, he kept working until midnight.
questions.explanation: 'Although' introduces a contrast — like 'but', but at the start of a sentence.

questions.question: The jacket was very expensive. ___, we decided to buy it.
questions.explanation: 'However' shows contrast between two sentences. It usually has a comma after it.

questions.question: ___ the heavy rain, we still had a great picnic.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' is followed by a noun or -ing form: 'despite the rain', 'despite raining'.

questions.question: I'm saving my pocket money ___ I can buy a new game.
questions.explanation: 'So that' shows purpose — the reason WHY we do something.

questions.question: My brother is ___ student in his whole class.
questions.explanation: For short adjectives, the superlative is 'the + adjective + -est'.

questions.question: My new school isn't ___ big as my old one.
questions.explanation: '(not) as ___ as' is used to compare two things — to say they are equal or not equal.

questions.question: Put the words in the correct order to make a question:
questions.explanation: Question word + 'have' + subject + past participle: 'How long have you known her?'
questions.correctSentence: How long have you known her ?

questions.question: Put the words in order to make a sentence:
questions.explanation: With present perfect, 'never' goes between 'have' and the past participle.
questions.correctSentence: I have never been to Italy

questions.question: Put the words in order to make a sentence:
questions.explanation: Second conditional: 'If + past simple, … would + verb' — for imaginary situations.
questions.correctSentence: If I had time I would sleep more

questions.question: Put the words in order to make a sentence:
questions.explanation: In reported questions, we use normal word order (subject + verb), not question order.
questions.correctSentence: She asked me where I lived

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: 'For + length of time' with present perfect describes a state continuing from past to now.
options: I'm living here for five years.
I have lived here for five years.
I lived here for five years.
I live here since five years.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: In the first conditional, never use 'will' in the 'if' part — only in the main part.
options: If I will see him, I'll tell him.
If I see him, I'll tell him.
If I saw him, I'll tell him.
If I see him, I tell him.

questions.question: My uncle decided to ___ smoking last year. Now he's much healthier.
questions.explanation: 'Give up' means to stop doing something — usually a habit.

questions.question: Could you ___ my dog while I'm on holiday next week?
questions.explanation: 'Look after' means to take care of someone or something.

questions.question: I haven't eaten anything all day — I'm absolutely ___!
questions.explanation: 'Starving' is a strong word for very hungry. Strong adjectives often go with 'absolutely'.

questions.question: Where did Mia go on her school trip?
questions.explanation: The text says: 'she went on a school trip to a wildlife park with her classmates.'
questions.reading: Mia is 12 years old and lives in a small town near the mountains. Last summer, she went on a school trip to a wildlife park with her classmates. They spent three days walking through forests, watching birds, and learning about different animals. The guide, Mr. Brown, was very funny and told them many interesting stories. Although the weather wasn't great — it rained on the second day — Mia had a wonderful time. She especially loved seeing the deer in the early morning. When she got home, she decided she wanted to become a vet when she grows up. Now, she reads books about animals every evening before bed.

questions.question: How did Mia feel about the trip overall?
questions.explanation: Even though it rained, she 'had a wonderful time'. 'Although' shows the rain didn't ruin it for her.
questions.reading: Mia is 12 years old and lives in a small town near the mountains. Last summer, she went on a school trip to a wildlife park with her classmates. They spent three days walking through forests, watching birds, and learning about different animals. The guide, Mr. Brown, was very funny and told them many interesting stories. Although the weather wasn't great — it rained on the second day — Mia had a wonderful time. She especially loved seeing the deer in the early morning. When she got home, she decided she wanted to become a vet when she grows up. Now, she reads books about animals every evening before bed.
options: She was bored most of the time
She didn't enjoy it because of the rain
She was scared of the animals

questions.question: What does Mia want to be when she grows up?
questions.explanation: The text says she 'decided she wanted to become a vet' — that's a doctor for animals.
questions.reading: Mia is 12 years old and lives in a small town near the mountains. Last summer, she went on a school trip to a wildlife park with her classmates. They spent three days walking through forests, watching birds, and learning about different animals. The guide, Mr. Brown, was very funny and told them many interesting stories. Although the weather wasn't great — it rained on the second day — Mia had a wonderful time. She especially loved seeing the deer in the early morning. When she got home, she decided she wanted to become a vet when she grows up. Now, she reads books about animals every evening before bed.

questions.question: You like pizza, ___?
questions.explanation: For a positive sentence ('You like pizza'), we use a NEGATIVE question tag ('don't you?').
